operating_state: RTX generated $18.3 billion in Q2 2023 revenue, up 12% year-over-year, driven by organic growth across all segments. Commercial aerospace recovery continued with Collins and Pratt & Whitney aftermarket sales reflecting higher flight hours and volume recovery. Defense sales increased driven by higher AMRAAM program volumes following a second quarter award and increased Advanced Technology program sales from classified contracts awarded in 2022.
cost_structure: Cost of sales increased to 79.3% of revenue from 78.8% in Q2 2022, primarily driven by volume increases and higher production costs across segments. Selling, general and administrative expenses rose to 8.9% of sales including a customer insolvency charge at Pratt & Whitney. FAS/CAS operating adjustment decreased due to changes in Raytheon domestic pension plans effective December 2022.
material_events: RTX changed its legal name from Raytheon Technologies Corporation effective July 17, 2023, and streamlined from four business segments to three effective July 1, 2023, combining RIS and RMD into a single Raytheon segment. Pratt & Whitney announced accelerated fleet inspections for PW1100G-JM engines due to a rare powdered metal condition, requiring approximately 200 engine removals by mid-September 2023. The company issued long-term debt and repurchased $1.2 billion of shares in the first half.
macro_exposures: China added RMD to its unreliable entities list in February 2023 with potential fines equal to twice the value of Taiwan arms sales since September 2020, and sanctioned CEO Gregory Hayes in connection with Taiwan foreign military sales. Ongoing supply chain constraints include materials shortages, labor shortages particularly for specialized engineering personnel, and delivery delays compounded by geopolitical tensions and Russia-Ukraine conflict sanctions.
forward_outlook: Management expects to fully return to or exceed pre-pandemic levels as the company exits 2023, driven by fundamental air travel demand recovery and continued commercial aerospace aftermarket growth as flight hours normalize. Defense bookings support future revenue visibility, with total backlog reaching $185 billion. The company maintains remaining share repurchase authorization under the December 2022 program.
uncertainty_profile: Pratt & Whitney faces significant near-term operational disruption from required PW1100G-JM fleet inspections over the next nine to twelve months. Contract liabilities include advance payments from a Middle East customer where regulatory approvals are no longer expected, potentially requiring refunds. Geopolitical tensions create ongoing risks to international sales through potential trade restrictions, sanctions, and regulatory changes affecting defense and commercial aerospace businesses.
